event: Stone Age tools and evidence of early human habitation are left along the Gambia River valley by Middle Stone Age peoples.
Hunter-gatherer communities exploit the river's fish and shellfish, leaving extensive shell middens along the lower Gambia estuary.
Agricultural and iron-working communities settle the region, cultivating cereals and herding livestock along the fertile river floodplain.
Senegambian stone circles, including those later at Wassu and Kerbatch, begin to be erected as burial monuments by local societies.
The Gambia River region falls within the sphere of the Ghana Empire's trans-Saharan trade networks linking it to North Africa.
The rise of the Mali Empire under Sundiata Keita brings the Gambia River area under Mandinka influence and gold-trade prosperity.
Mandinka migrants establish small kingdoms such as Niumi, Baddibu, and Kombo along both banks of the Gambia River.
Portuguese navigator Alvise Cadamosto, sailing for Prince Henry, explores the mouth of the Gambia River for Europe.
Cadamosto's expedition ventures upriver and encounters local Mandinka rulers, beginning sustained Portuguese contact.
Portuguese traders establish riverside settlements and begin exchanging goods, including the early traffic in enslaved Africans.
Portugal cedes its exclusive Gambia trading rights to English merchants under a treaty with the Portuguese pretender Dom Antonio.
Portuguese influence wanes as Dutch, English, and French traders increasingly compete for the river's commerce.
Duke Jakob of Courland establishes a Baltic colonial trading post on St. Andrew's Island (later James Island) in the river.
English forces under Robert Holmes seize the island from the Courlanders and rename it James Island after the Duke of York.
France establishes a competing trading post at Albreda on the north bank, intensifying Anglo-French rivalry on the river.
British trader Job ben Solomon (Ayuba Suleiman Diallo) is enslaved on the Gambia, later gaining freedom and fame in England.
Britain incorporates the Gambia River posts into the Crown colony of Senegambia, administered from Senegal.
The Treaty of Versailles confirms British control of the Gambia River while France retains Senegal.
Britain abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, and the Royal Navy begins patrolling the Gambia to suppress slaving.
Britain founds Bathurst (modern Banjul) on Banjul Island as a base to combat the slave trade and command the river mouth.
The settlement of Georgetown (Janjanbureh) is established upriver as a haven for liberated and recaptured Africans.
France cedes the Albreda trading post to Britain, ending the long French presence on the Gambia River's north bank.
The Gambia is administered as part of the British West African Settlements grouped under Sierra Leone.
The Gambia becomes a separate British colony with its own administration during the Scramble for Africa.
An Anglo-French convention fixes the boundaries of the Gambia as a narrow enclave following the river through French Senegal.
Britain formally declares a protectorate over the interior districts beyond the Bathurst colony.
Soninke-Marabout wars subside as British authority is consolidated over the chiefdoms of the protectorate.
An ordinance abolishes the legal status of slavery throughout the Gambia protectorate.
Groundnut (peanut) exports become the backbone of the colonial economy, dominating the country's trade.
U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt visits Bathurst during World War II, the first sitting American president to set foot in Africa.
A new constitution introduces a partly elected House of Representatives, expanding African political participation.
Dawda Jawara's People's Progressive Party wins elections and he becomes premier ahead of self-government.
The Gambia attains full internal self-government with Dawda Jawara as prime minister.
The Gambia gains independence from Britain on 18 February, with Dawda Jawara as prime minister and Elizabeth II as queen.
The Gambia joins the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
The Gambia becomes a republic on 24 April following a referendum, with Dawda Jawara as its first president.
A coup attempt against President Jawara is crushed with Senegalese military intervention, after heavy loss of life.
The Gambia and Senegal form the loose Senegambia Confederation to coordinate defense, foreign policy, and economic affairs.
The Senegambia Confederation collapses amid disputes over its scope and Gambian fears of Senegalese domination.
Lieutenant Yahya Jammeh seizes power in a bloodless military coup, ending Jawara's 29-year rule.
A new constitution is adopted and Jammeh wins a contested presidential election to formalize civilian rule.
Security forces kill student demonstrators in Banjul during protests, drawing condemnation of the Jammeh government.
President Jammeh withdraws the Gambia from the Commonwealth, denouncing it as a neo-colonial institution.
Jammeh declares the Gambia an Islamic republic, deepening international concern over governance.
Adama Barrow becomes president after Jammeh, having lost the 2016 election, is forced into exile under ECOWAS military pressure.
The Gambia rejoins the Commonwealth of Nations under President Barrow.
The Truth, Reconciliation and Reparations Commission begins televised hearings into abuses committed during Jammeh's rule.
Adama Barrow wins re-election in a peaceful vote, consolidating the country's democratic transition.
